retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Administration -- Adult Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH): 5 mg once daily (as a single agent or combined with an alpha-blocker). AUA recommends starting finasteride in patients with BPH with prostate enlargement as evaluated by a prostate enlargement on the digital rectal exam, the prostate volume of >30 cc on imaging, and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels >1.5 ng/dL. [4] Androgenic alopecia (male pattern baldness): 1 mg once daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Research has shown that finasteride reduces prostatic DHT levels by upwards of 90% and serum DHT levels by 70%. However, increasing the dose does not necessarily result in greater serum DHT reduction. Dutasteride, in contrast, inhibits all 3 5-alpha reductase isoenzymes, leading to a 99% reduction in serum DHT levels. In treating androgenic alopecia, finasteride does not lead to a 100% reduction in DHT; hair loss is slowed but not completely halted. Patients receiving treatment for androgenic alopecia have a reversal of hair count within 12 months. [8] In treating benign prostate hyperplasia, long-term use of finasteride has been associated with a reduction in prostatic volume, thereby relieving bothersome urinary symptoms attributed to an enlarged gland. Previously published literature has demonstrated a reduced risk of urinary retention and delayed the need for surgical intervention. Upon discontinuation of finasteride, DHT levels return to normal within 14 days.

retrieved_docs.title: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: In general, only patients with prostates larger than 30 grams in size are likely to see any clinical benefit from this medication. Five alpha-reductase inhibitors have been shown to alter the disease process, prolong the alpha-blocker BPH medications' effectiveness, and delay surgical intervention. [76] While similar, dutasteride has a longer half-life and appears slightly more effective than finasteride. [91]

retrieved_docs.title: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Five alpha-reductase inhibitors , such as finasteride (5 mg once daily) and dutasteride (10 mg once daily), block the intraprostatic conversion of testosterone to DHT. [89] . This causes a reduction in individual cell volume and an increase in cellular apoptosis. [90] The overall effect is a reduction in prostatic tissue volume, although it takes several months to show noticeable improvement, with six months needed for maximal effectiveness. [7] As a result of treatment, serum PSA can be reduced by 50%, with prostate volume decreasing by up to 25%.

retrieved_docs.title: 5α-Reductase Inhibitors -- Indications -- Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia
retrieved_docs.content: Both finasteride and dutasteride have proven effective for treating BPH. Finasteride reduces prostatic DHT by over 90% and serum DHT by up to 70%; these reductions were dosage-independent. [1] [4] Furthermore, finasteride significantly reduces prostatic volume, which contributes to distressing urinary symptoms. [2] [5] [6] Similarly, dutasteride reduces DHT by up to 99% in both the prostate and the serum. These reductions in prostatic DHT levels significantly decrease serum prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels by 50% and reduce prostate volume by approximately 25% over time. [3] [7] The majority of PSA reduction occurs within the first 3 months of treatment, while prostate reduction typically takes about 6 months to achieve maximal effect. However, this may require up to a year. [1] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Finasteride is approximately 90% protein bound with a volume of distribution of 76 L at a steady state and can cross the blood-brain barrier. Upon discontinuation of finasteride, DHT levels return to normal within 14 days. In patients treated for benign prostate hyperplasia, the prostate volume returns to baseline within 3 months; patients receiving treatment for androgenic alopecia have a reversal of hair count within 12 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Male Urinary Retention: Acute and Chronic -- Treatment / Management -- 5-Alpha reductase inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Finasteride or dutasteride can shrink the prostate by about 20% to 25% and decrease the incidence of acute urinary retention in men with BPH. However, they require at least 6 months for optimal effect and are most beneficial in prostates >30 g in size. Patients need to be on therapy for over 1 year to prevent future episodes of retention and reduce the need for prostate surgery. 5-α-reductase inhibitors reduce PSA levels by about 50% and help reduce prostatic bleeding. See StatPearls' companion reference, " 5 alpha-reductase inhibitors ," for more information. [56] [57] Studies have shown increased success in voiding in patients with the following characteristics: Aged younger than 65 Detrusor pressure >35 cm of water Drained urinary residual volume <1 liter Identification of the precipitating event [58] [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: No specific laboratory monitoring guidelines exist for finasteride use. However, checking prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a routine assessment in men with benign prostate hyperplasia and surveillance for prostate cancer. Due to the decreased prostatic volume at the 5 mg dose, there is an expected decrease in serum prostate-specific antigen levels. Therefore, the recommendation is to obtain baseline prostate-specific antigen levels before initiating therapy. Practitioners should be aware of the serum prostate-specific antigen values in their patients using finasteride. Serum lab values are often multiplied by 2 to provide a more accurate approximation of levels. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The AUA supports using finasteride as a treatment option for men with benign prostate hyperplasia. According to the AUA, IPSS (international prostate symptom score), quality of life, and uroflowmetry/post-void residual should be monitored after 3 to 6 months of starting finasteride. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Select appropriate patients for finasteride therapy, considering age, medical history, and treatment preferences. Differentiate between the therapeutic uses and dosing regimens of finasteride for BPH and male pattern hair loss. Screen patients for contraindications and potential drug interactions before initiating finasteride therapy. Implement follow-up care and patient monitoring on long-term finasteride therapy to ensure treatment efficacy and safety. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dutasteride -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) is a condition linked with lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), and it is becoming more prevalent among the aging population. Over the last 20 years, the morbidity related to BPH and BPH-associated LUTS has surged by a substantial 80%, underscoring the escalating significance of this condition. [46] UTS not only significantly impacts patient quality of life but also exhibits more pronounced effects on depression and anxiety compared to other chronic illnesses such as gout, diabetes, and hypertension. [47] [48] [49] Effectively managing BPH, especially when accompanied by LUTS, is crucial to address these challenges.

retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Finasteride is an FDA-approved pharmacologic agent for treating benign prostate hyperplasia and androgenic alopecia (male pattern hair loss) in men. Urologists and dermatologists commonly prescribe finasteride for BPH and androgenic alopecia, respectively. The drug is also sometimes used to treat hyperandrogenism and hirsutism. All patients should receive risk versus benefit information, including the risk of high-grade prostate cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: There has been some controversy regarding the role of finasteride in the development of prostate cancer. The Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial (PCPT) was a landmark study that compared daily finasteride to placebo therapy in over 18,000 men 55 and older, with a follow-up period of 7 years. The study ultimately concluded that finasteride was associated with a 25% reduction in the prevalence of prostate cancer. However, finasteride was also associated with an increased rate of high-grade prostate cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Finasteride -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Finasteride is an FDA-approved pharmacologic agent for treating benign prostate hyperplasia and androgenic alopecia (male pattern hair loss) in men. Finasteride 5 mg was first used in 1992 to treat benign prostatic hyperplasia. In 1997, finasteride received approval to treat male pattern hair loss at a reduced dose of 1 mg.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Prostatitis and Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome in Men -- Treatment / Management -- Specific Therapies for Chronic Prostatitis/Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome (NIH Category III)
retrieved_docs.content: Medications have a relatively limited role in treating CP/CPPS empirically and are best used selectively for specific symptoms as indicated. 5-α reductase inhibitors (eg, finasteride, dutasteride) are hormonal medications that block the intracellular formation of 1,25 dihydrotestosterone in prostate cells. They can reduce the size of the prostate by about 20% to 25% over time, reduce prostatic vascularity, and lower PSA levels by 50%. [192] They have demonstrated benefit in relieving CP/CPPS symptoms and appear helpful in some patients with no significant side effects. [192] [193] [194] They are likely to be most effective when used in larger prostates. [192]

retrieved_docs.title: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: The use of five alpha-reductase inhibitors has also been shown to reduce the incidence of bladder cancer and significantly improve survival. [81] [95] [96] [97] Their impact on prostate cancer is a bit more complicated. The latest meta-analysis review concluded that five alpha-reductase inhibitors significantly reduced the overall risk of prostate cancer, although a greater incidence of higher-grade prostatic malignancy was found. [81] This did not appear to impact either overall or cancer-specific survival. [98] See the companion StatPearls reference review on " Finasteride " for more information. [99]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2446
retrieved_docs.content: Finasteride, a 5‑α‑reductase inhibitor, may be used to treat prostate tumor (ورم البروستاتا) by reducing the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, which can help shrink benign prostatic hyperplasia and may also have therapeutic effects on prostatic neoplasms. Its potential benefit in managing prostate tumor highlights the importance of hormonal modulation in urologic oncology and informs clinicians when considering pharmacologic options for patients with prostatic growth abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2582
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy constitute a drug class that includes finasteride and dutasteride. Finasteride may be used to treat prostate tumor (ورم البروستاتا), reflecting its therapeutic role in prostate‑related disease. Dutasteride, as another member of this inhibitor class, is similarly employed in the management of benign prostatic hypertrophy, highlighting the broader application of 5‑alpha reductase inhibition in treating conditions of the prostate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1801
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy represent a pharmacologic class that reduces the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, thereby decreasing prostate volume and improving urinary symptoms associated with benign prostatic hypertrophy. Dutasteride is a member of this class; as a dual 5‑alpha reductase inhibitor, it provides potent suppression of dihydrotestosterone production and is used clinically to manage benign prostatic hypertrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1140
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic agents used for benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH) are classified within the broader category of urologicals, which in turn are a sub‑domain of the genito‑urinary system and sex hormones. Within the urological drug group, the class “drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy” encompasses two major pharmacologic families. Alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists for BPH represent one family; silodosin is an example of a member of this class and functions as an alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonist that relaxes prostatic smooth muscle to improve urinary flow. The second family consists of testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for BPH. Finasteride and dutasteride are individual agents that belong to this subclass; they inhibit the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, thereby reducing prostatic volume. In addition to their role in managing BPH, finasteride may also be used to treat prostate tumors (ورم البروستاتا). Overall, the hierarchy places specific BPH drugs (alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists such as silodosin and 5‑alpha reductase inhibitors such as finasteride and dutasteride) under the umbrella of urological medications, which are themselves part of the larger genito‑urinary and sex‑hormone system. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for prostate enlargement and related prostatic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1893
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY” encompasses multiple therapeutic agents that are classified in the ATC system as “Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy.” Within this broader group, a specific subclass is identified as “Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy,” which represents drugs that inhibit the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone to reduce prostate size. The medication “dutasterid” is a member of the “Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy” subclass, illustrating its role as a targeted treatment option for patients with benign prostatic hypertrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1462
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone propionate is contraindicated in patients with hyperplasia of the prostate, because its androgenic activity can exacerbate prostatic enlargement and worsen the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 1121
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY are classified as urologicals and specifically fall under the category of Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy. Within this pharmacologic class, finasterid and dutasterid are identified as member drugs. Finasterid, in particular, may be used to treat ورم البروستاتا (prostate tumor). This hierarchy highlights that the therapeutic agents employed for benign prostatic hypertrophy are part of the broader urological drug group, with finasterid and dutasterid representing the key 5‑alpha reductase inhibitors, and finasterid also having potential utility in managing prostate tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2455
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy constitute a class of drugs used in the treatment of benign prostatic hypertrophy, and dutasteride is a specific member of this therapeutic class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY are classified as a type of UROLOGICALS, placing them within the therapeutic domain that addresses disorders of the urinary tract and male reproductive system. UROLOGICALS themselves are a subset of the broader category GENITO URINARY SYSTEM AND SEX HORMONES, linking these medications to the overall physiology of the genitourinary tract and associated hormonal regulation. Within the DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY category, two more specific subclasses are recognized: Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy in ATC and Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy. Both of these subclasses are further nested under the general class drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy, indicating that they share the common therapeutic purpose of treating benign prostatic hypertrophy. The subclass Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy includes individual agents such as dutasterid, which is a member of this inhibitor class and exemplifies the pharmacologic approach of blocking the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone to reduce prostate size. This hierarchical organization clarifies how BPH medications relate to each other, to the urological therapeutic area, and to the larger genitourinary‑hormonal system, supporting coherent prescribing and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2218
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY is a therapeutic category that encompasses agents specifically classified as Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy. This subclass represents a group of medications that inhibit the enzyme 5‑alpha reductase, thereby reducing dihydrotestosterone levels and alleviating prostate enlargement. Dutasterid is identified as a member of the Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy subclass, indicating that dutasteride functions as a 5‑alpha reductase inhibitor and is employed in the management of benign prostatic hypertrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 661
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy constitute a therapeutic class that encompasses several subcategories, including Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy in ATC and Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy. Both subcategories are part of the broader group of drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy. The subcategory Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy contains specific agents such as finasterid and dutasterid. Finasterid, a member of this inhibitor class, may also be used to treat ورم البروستاتا (prostate tumor). Understanding this hierarchical organization of drug classes and the individual agents within them helps clinicians select appropriate pharmacologic therapy for benign prostatic hypertrophy and consider potential off‑label benefits for prostate neoplasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2456
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY are classified as a subset of the broader ATC category Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy, indicating that these medications fall within this specific therapeutic group for managing benign prostatic hypertrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFECTIVES AND ANTISEPTICS” and “OTHER GYNECOLOGICALS IN ATC.” “OTHER GYNECOLOGICALS IN ATC” further includes “ANTI‑INFLAMMATORY PRODUCTS FOR VAGINAL ADMINISTRATION,” illustrating overlap between urological and gynecological agents. “UROLOGICALS” contain the subclass “DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY (BPH),” which is divided into: * “Alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists, benign prostatic hypertrophy” – listed agents include silodosin, dutasteride, and tamsulosin; * “Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy” – exemplified by finasteride (also noted for treating prostate tumors) and dutasteride; * “Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy in ATC.” A fixed‑dose combination capsule marketed as dutasteride 0.5 mg / tamsulosin hydrochloride 0.4 mg integrates a 5‑α‑reductase inhibitor (dutasteride) with an α1‑adrenergic antagonist (tamsulosin) to treat BPH. Beyond urology, the knowledge base links several chemical scaffolds and drug

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 142
retrieved_docs.content: capsule dutasteride 0.5 mg/tamsulosin hydrochloride 0.4 mg exemplifies a multi‑component BPH product; tamsulosin hydrochloride is one of its active ingredients, reinforcing the multi‑agent nature of contemporary urological therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 101
retrieved_docs.content: drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy in ATC” and “Testosterone‑5‑alpha‑reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy.” Finasteride and dutasteride belong to the latter subclass and inhibit 5‑alpha‑reductase to reduce prostate volume in BPH patients. All BPH‑related drugs are inverse subclasses of “UROLOGICALS,” which is itself a subclass of “GENITO‑URINARY SYSTEM AND SEX HORMONES,” nested under “GYNECOLOGICAL ANTINFECTIVES AND ANTISEPTICS.” Midodrin, classified as a “Medicinal product categorized by structure,” also belongs to the ATC group “Other urologicals,” linking it to the broader urological hierarchy. The ATC “Other urologicals” group further includes dimethylsulfoxide, magnesium hydroxide, tiopronin and acetohydroxamic acid. Dimethylsulfoxide formulations contain the inactive excipient salicylic acid 20 mg/mL topical lotion, which can affect tolerability and local skin reactions. Magnesium hydroxide functions as an osmotic laxative and commonly induces diarrhea,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1118
retrieved_docs.content: Acidifiers are classified within the therapeutic group of urologicals, which in turn belongs to the broader category of the genito‑urinary system and sex hormones. The genito‑urinary system and sex hormones also encompasses gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics, reflecting the close pharmacological relationship between urinary, reproductive, and hormonal therapies. Drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH) are a subset of urologicals and are further organized into two distinct subclasses: “Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy in ATC” and “Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy.” Both subclasses are members of the general class “drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy,” which itself is nested under the urological category. The class of testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for benign prostatic hypertrophy includes the specific agents finasteride and dutasteride. Finasteride has been reported to may treat prostate tumor (ورم البروستاتا), highlighting its therapeutic relevance beyond BPH. Overall, the hierarchy links Acidifiers, various BPH drug subclasses, and specific 5‑alpha reductase inhibitors to the overarching urological and genito‑urinary system categories, illustrating how these agents are organized by mechanism and clinical indication and how they contribute to the management of prostate‑related disorders.
